topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a familiar avatar smiling back at me. 'Hey, random coincidence! I live in downtown Meridian too!' My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that burned in my chest. I stared at the screen, my fingers hovering over the keyboard, unable to type anything. Meridian. That was the city I’d only ever seen in blurry travel photos. My heart began a frantic drumming against my ribs, a rapid, uneven rhythm. I quickly minimized the chat window, forcing myself to look around my small apartment, the familiar walls suddenly feeling too close, too confining. Every creak of the floorboards sounded like a footstep outside my door. I kept checking the time, the digital display mocking the slow, creeping passage of minutes.

[story 2]

I reread the message three times, the words swimming before my eyes. 'Wait, you're in Meridian?' A cold sweat slicked my palms as I gripped my phone so tightly my knuckles turned white. The air in the room seemed to thicken, heavy and suffocating. I stood up abruptly, pacing the narrow confines of my living room, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I walked to the window, peering out into the unfamiliar cityscape, the towering buildings seeming to loom larger, more imposing than before. A strange tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I felt a distinct urge to flee, to bolt for any exit, but my feet remained rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the sudden, overwhelming reality of the connection.

[story 3]

When the confirmation finally registered, a wave of nausea washed over me. I dropped my phone onto the desk with a clatter that echoed loudly in the silence of my room. My gaze darted around the room, cataloging every shadow, every corner, searching for an escape route that didn't exist. My hands trembled uncontrollably as I reached for the remote, needing something solid to focus on. The silence stretched, taut and brittle, punctuated only by the frantic thumping inside my own ears. I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, a sensation that made focusing on anything else impossible. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the effort painful, as I waited for the next digital intrusion.
